title: SQL Injection in Product Search
description: User-supplied search criteria is interpolated directly into a raw SQL query without parameterization. An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL to extract schema, dump user credentials, or manipulate data. This bypasses Sequelize's ORM protections by using the raw query interface.

title: SQL Injection in Login Authentication
description: The login route builds a SQL WHERE clause by directly interpolating req.body.email into the query string. An attacker can use a classic ' OR 1=1-- payload to bypass authentication entirely and log in as any user including admin.

title: NoSQL Injection via $where in Product Reviews
description: The product ID from the URL is concatenated directly into a MongoDB $where clause, which executes arbitrary JavaScript on the server. An attacker can inject JavaScript expressions to bypass the product filter and read all reviews, or cause denial of service with infinite loops.

title: NoSQL Injection in Order Tracking
description: The orderId path parameter is interpolated into a MongoDB $where JavaScript expression without sanitization. An attacker can craft a payload that evaluates true for all documents, exposing every order in the collection.

title: NoSQL Mass Update Injection in Review Update
description: The update operation uses {multi: true} with a user-supplied _id, allowing an attacker to supply a wildcard or always-true _id filter that matches all review documents and overwrites their messages. There is no check that the review being updated belongs to the authenticated user.

title: Remote Code Execution via eval() on User Input
description: When a username matches the pattern /#{(.*)}/  the captured group is passed directly to eval(). An attacker who sets their username to #{require('child_process').execSync('id')} achieves arbitrary command execution on the server with the Node.js process's privileges.

title: Server-Side Template Injection via Pug
description: User-controlled username is substituted into a Pug template string that is then compiled and rendered server-side. An attacker can inject Pug code to read arbitrary files, execute OS commands, or dump process environment variables.

title: XXE via Unsafe XML Parsing with noent:true
description: Uploaded XML files are parsed with the noent option enabled, which resolves external XML entities. An attacker can craft a document with an external entity pointing to file:///etc/passwd or an internal network resource to read arbitrary server files or perform SSRF.

title: Unsafe YAML Deserialization
description: Uploaded YAML files are processed with yaml.load() rather than yaml.safeLoad()/yaml.load() with a safe schema. Depending on the js-yaml version, this can allow instantiation of arbitrary JavaScript objects or cause denial of service via a YAML bomb with deeply nested anchors.

title: Path Traversal / Zip Slip in File Upload
description: Zip archive entries are extracted after checking that the resolved path includes the current working directory, but the check uses includes() which can be fooled by crafted paths. An attacker can place a payload zip entry with a path like ../../app.js to overwrite application files outside the intended upload directory.

title: Open Redirect via Substring Allowlist Check
description: The redirect URL is validated by checking whether it contains an allowlisted domain string rather than comparing the host exactly. An attacker can bypass this by crafting a URL like https://evil.com/?q=allowedsite.com that passes the includes() check but redirects to a phishing page.

title: Open Redirect Allowlist Bypass in insecurity.ts
description: The isUrl helper uses url.includes(allowedUrl) for validation rather than parsing the URL and comparing the hostname. This allows bypassing the redirect protection using URLs that contain the allowlisted domain as a query parameter or path segment.

title: Wildcard CORS Policy
description: The application uses cors() with default settings, which sets Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * for all responses. Combined with permissive cookie handling this can enable cross-origin requests from arbitrary domains to access authenticated API responses.

title: Hardcoded RSA Private Key
description: A full RSA private key is embedded in plaintext source code. Anyone with read access to the repository can use this key to forge JWT tokens, sign arbitrary data, or impersonate the server. The private key cannot be rotated without a code change.

title: MD5 Used for Password Hashing
description: Passwords are hashed with MD5, a fast cryptographic hash with no salt. MD5 password hashes are trivially cracked using rainbow tables or GPU-accelerated brute force in seconds. A modern password hashing function like bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2 must be used instead.

title: Hardcoded HMAC Secret
description: The HMAC signing secret 'pa4qacea4VK9t9nGv7yZtwmj' is hardcoded in source code. Any attacker who reads the source can compute valid HMAC signatures for arbitrary data, undermining the integrity protection provided by the HMAC.

title: Severely Outdated express-jwt (0.1.3)
description: express-jwt version 0.1.3 is from 2015 and contains multiple known security vulnerabilities including algorithm confusion attacks that allow attackers to forge tokens by switching to the 'none' algorithm. Versions before 6.x are affected by CVE-2020-15084.

title: Outdated jsonwebtoken (0.4.0) with Algorithm Confusion
description: jsonwebtoken 0.4.0 is affected by the algorithm=none vulnerability (CVE-2015-9235) where an attacker can craft a token with alg:none and no signature to bypass JWT verification entirely. This is a critical authentication bypass.

title: Outdated sanitize-html (1.4.2) with XSS Bypass
description: sanitize-html 1.4.2 contains multiple XSS bypass vulnerabilities that were patched in later versions. Crafted HTML payloads can evade the sanitizer and result in stored or reflected XSS in any page that renders sanitized user content.

title: SSRF in Profile Image URL Upload
description: The profile image URL endpoint fetches a user-supplied URL without validating the host or scheme. An attacker can supply internal URLs like http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ to access cloud instance metadata, internal services, or perform port scanning of the internal network.

title: IDOR in Basket Item Access
description: The basket ID ownership check compares the authenticated user's basket ID against the basket IDs in the request using loose numeric comparison. Type coercion or crafted request parameters may allow an attacker to access or modify another user's basket contents.

title: IDOR in Data Export Endpoint
description: The data export route uses req.body.UserId to determine which user's data to export without verifying that the supplied UserId matches the authenticated session user. An attacker can supply an arbitrary UserId to download another user's personal data including order history and memories.

title: Hardcoded Test Credentials in Source Code
description: Default credentials such as admin@localhost/admin123 are present in source code comments or initialization logic. These well-known credentials are trivially guessable and enable unauthorized admin access to any deployment that has not changed them.

title: Password Exposed in Query String
description: The current password is read from the URL query string (req.query.current) rather than the request body. Query string parameters are logged in web server access logs, browser history, proxy logs, and the Referer header, resulting in inadvertent credential exposure.

title: Insecure Password Reset via Security Questions
description: Password reset relies solely on a security question answer with no rate limiting on guesses. Security question answers are often publicly discoverable, and without rate limiting an attacker can brute force the answer space to reset any user's password.

title: Unsafe-eval in Content Security Policy
description: The profile page sets a Content-Security-Policy that includes script-src 'unsafe-eval', explicitly permitting eval() and related functions. This negates a key XSS defense layer and is required to support the SSTI vulnerability in the same file.

title: Information Disclosure via Verbose Error Messages
description: Internal error details including stack traces and system paths are returned to the client in error responses. Attackers can use this information for reconnaissance to identify server-side frameworks, file paths, and application internals.

title: Prometheus Metrics Endpoint Publicly Accessible
description: The /metrics endpoint exposes Prometheus application and runtime metrics without authentication. An unauthenticated attacker can use this data to enumerate application internals, active user counts, endpoint names, and response time patterns for targeted attacks.

title: No CSRF Token Protection on State-Changing Requests
description: State-changing routes rely on Origin/Referer header checks rather than unpredictable CSRF tokens. These headers can be absent or spoofed in certain browser and proxy configurations, leaving the application vulnerable to cross-site request forgery attacks.

title: Unsanitized JSONP Callback Parameter
description: The callback query parameter is passed to res.jsonp() without sanitization. If the callback name is not restricted to safe identifiers an attacker can inject script-like payloads that execute in browsers that process the JSONP response.

title: Cookies Missing Secure and HttpOnly Flags
description: Session and authentication cookies are set without the Secure flag (preventing transmission over HTTP) and may lack the HttpOnly flag (allowing JavaScript access). This enables session hijacking over unencrypted connections and cookie theft via XSS.

title: Missing HTTP Security Headers
description: The application does not set X-Content-Type-Options, Strict-Transport-Security, or a restrictive default Content-Security-Policy. The absence of these headers exposes users to MIME sniffing attacks, downgrade attacks, and broader XSS attack surfaces.

title: No Rate Limiting on Authentication Endpoints
description: Login and password-reset endpoints lack per-IP or per-account rate limiting. An attacker can automate credential stuffing or brute-force password guessing against any account without triggering lockout or throttling.

title: Unauthenticated Access to Product Review Queries
description: The product reviews endpoint does not require authentication before executing the database query. Combined with the NoSQL injection vulnerability this means unauthenticated attackers can also exploit the injection to access all review data.

title: Insecure Temporary File Handling in Upload Processing
description: Temporary files are written to the shared system temp directory with predictable filenames. On multi-user systems this creates race conditions where a local attacker can read or replace temporary files between creation and processing.

title: User Enumeration via Timing Differences
description: Authentication queries execute database lookups that take measurably different time depending on whether the email address matches an existing account. An attacker can use this timing oracle to enumerate valid usernames before targeting them for brute force.

title: Missing HSTS Header
description: The application does not set a Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) header, so browsers will not enforce HTTPS for subsequent visits. This allows network attackers to downgrade connections to HTTP and intercept credentials or session tokens.

title: Plaintext Password Storage in Reset Flow
description: The password reset route calls user.update({ password: newPassword }) without pre-hashing the new password before storage. If the ORM hook that applies hashing is absent or bypassed, passwords are stored in cleartext in the database.
